metadata.summary: Routing-layer methodology for classifying Python projects by domain (web/data/ML/automation), selecting toolchain (uv, ruff, mypy), and deciding optimization strategy. Use as first-pass router before loading framework-specific methodology.

content.title: Python Ecosystem Overview
content.summary: A routing-layer methodology for classifying a Python project's domain (web / data / ML / automation / embedded), selecting the right toolchain (Python version, package manager, linter, type checker), and routing to the correct framework-specific methodology. Use this as a first-pass router — not as implementation instructions. Once domain and stack are locked, load the specific methodology.

why.p: Python spans incompatible domains. Pulling all of PyTorch + Django + Celery into a simple API project is a common LLM over-reach. The right toolchain (uv, ruff, mypy) is not obvious from training data alone — agents default to outdated combos (pip + black + isort + flake8). Routing correctly at the start saves rework and prevents framework-mixing bugs.

list.item: Routing "we need a Python solution for X" to the correct domain and methodology
Onboarding a new repo: surveying imports to map them to a domain bucket
Picking Python version, package manager, and lint/typecheck stack at project bootstrap
Auditing a codebase for outdated tooling (black + isort + flake8 → ruff; pip → uv)
Deciding whether a hot loop should be rewritten in Rust (only after profiling)
Inside a feature task already scoped to a specific framework — load the specific methodology instead
Choosing between Python web frameworks — route to python-web-frameworks which has the decision matrix
Performance work where a real profile already exists — this overview's optimization table is qualitative only

rule.statement: Use Python 3.12+ for new projects. Lock the version in pyproject.toml (requires-python = ">=3.12") AND in .python-version (for uv/pyenv).
rule.rationale: Python 3.12 is fully supported with performance improvements and cleaner f-strings. 3.13 free-threading is experimental and not production-ready as of 2026.

rule.statement: Do not recommend Python 3.13 free-threading for production. It is experimental — keep GIL-Python until 3.14+ default builds support it.
rule.rationale: Free-threaded Python 3.13 has known correctness issues in CPython extensions and is not stable enough for production workloads.

rule.statement: Use uv for new projects. One package manager per project — never mix uv and poetry or pip-tools in the same repo.
rule.rationale: uv is 10-100x faster than pip for installs and handles virtualenvs automatically. Mixing managers creates conflicting lockfiles.

rule.statement: Treat uv.lock and poetry.lock as source of truth. Regenerating without committing the lockfile breaks colleagues.
rule.rationale: Lockfiles pin exact transitive dependency versions; uncommitted changes mean different environments on different machines.

example.title: Modern Python project bootstrap
example.code: uv init my-project --python 3.12 uv add fastapi uvicorn uv add --dev ruff mypy pytest pytest-cov uv run pytest -q

rule.statement: Use ruff for both linting and formatting. It replaces black, isort, and flake8. Configure rule set explicitly — never use select = ["ALL"].
rule.rationale: ruff ALL enables hundreds of rules including opinionated ones that produce false positives. Curated set per AGENTS.md: E, F, I, B, C4, UP, SIM, T20.

rule.statement: Pick one type checker for CI (mypy or pyright). Both in CI wastes compute. Use the other optionally in the IDE.
rule.rationale: Running both in CI doubles type-check time for marginal benefit. The two tools have slightly different rule sets; CI needs a single pass/fail signal.

section.name: domains

rule.statement: Classify the project's primary domain before loading any framework-specific methodology. Use imports and dependency list as signals.
rule.rationale: Python spans incompatible domains. Loading the wrong methodology produces irrelevant patterns and wastes context.

list.item: web-django — imports: django, rest_framework, drf. Admin panel, ORM, full-stack. Route to: django-models/, django-pytest/
web-fastapi — imports: fastapi, pydantic, sqlalchemy. Async API, ML serving. Route to: python-fastapi/
web-flask — imports: flask. Prototype, internal tool. Route to: python-web-frameworks for decision, then Flask specifics
data-science — imports: pandas, polars, numpy, jupyter. Route to: data science methodologies
ml — imports: torch, tensorflow, sklearn, transformers, langchain. Route to: ml-engineer skill
automation — imports: airflow, prefect, selenium, ansible. Route to: automation methodologies
embedded/system — Rust + PyO3, Cython, cffi. Route to: performance methodologies

rule.statement: For web projects: if framework is already chosen, load only the framework-specific methodology. Load python-web-frameworks only when the choice is still open.
rule.rationale: python-web-frameworks is a routing layer — it adds overhead when the choice is already made.

section.p: Web landscape 2025-2026: FastAPI dominates new API projects; Django remains strong for admin-heavy apps. Flask for prototypes and internal tools.
Data landscape: pandas for manipulation; polars gaining for large datasets (Rust performance); NumPy for numeric computing.
ML/AI landscape: PyTorch for research; TensorFlow for production mobile; scikit-learn for classical ML; Hugging Face for NLP; pydantic-ai for type-safe LLM interactions.
Automation landscape: Apache Airflow for workflow orchestration; Prefect for modern workflow management; Selenium/PyAutoGUI for UI automation.

section.name: performance

rule.statement: Always profile before optimizing. Use cProfile or py-spy to identify the actual bottleneck before choosing an optimization strategy.
rule.rationale: The optimization strategy table's speedup numbers are qualitative. Agents that quote "10-1000x" without a profile recommend the wrong strategy constantly. CPU-bound optimizations do nothing for I/O-bound code.

example.title: Profiling techniques
example.code:
# CPU profiling python -m cProfile -s cumulative script.py | head -20
# Line-level profiling pip install line_profiler kernproof -l -v script.py
# Sampling profiler (minimal overhead, works on running processes) pip install py-spy py-spy top --pid <pid>

section.p: Optimization strategy table by speedup/complexity: Algorithm optimization (10-1000x, Low complexity) — always try first. NumPy vectorization (10-100x, Low) — numeric operations only. Numba JIT (10-100x, Low) — numeric, CPU-bound only. Cython (10-50x, Medium) — tight loops. Rust + PyO3 (10-100x, High) — critical paths with distribution complexity. PyPy (2-10x, Low) — pure Python; not compatible with all C extensions.

rule.statement: Rust+PyO3 integration is only justified after profiling confirms a CPU-bound hot path. Set up maturin with abi3 for a single wheel covering Python 3.10-3.14.
rule.rationale: Build and distribution complexity (manylinux wheels, maturin, ABI3) is substantial. FFI boundary overhead kills the win for small-payload functions.

rule.statement: Do not recommend Numba or Cython for I/O-bound code. They speed up CPU operations only — applying them to network or disk I/O loops yields zero improvement.
rule.rationale: Agents routinely recommend Numba for any "slow" code without checking whether it is CPU or I/O bound.

antipattern.description: Swapping poetry/pip for uv in projects with corporate proxies or private indexes.
antipattern.reason: uv's --index-url semantics differ from pip's; private Artifactory/Nexus/Azure Artifacts setups may require pip-specific config that uv does not support identically.

antipattern.description: Upgrading an existing Python 3.10/3.11 codebase to 3.12+ without checking dependency compatibility.
antipattern.reason: numpy<2, tensorflow, and psycopg2/psycopg3 have Python version lockstep issues. Always audit with pip-audit or safety before upgrading.
ALL enables hundreds of opinionated rules producing false positives. Curate: E, F, I, B, C4, UP, SIM, T20 per project AGENTS.md.

antipattern.description: Replacing requirements.txt with pyproject.toml + uv.lock without updating CI/Dockerfile to read the lockfile.
antipattern.reason: CI keeps installing the old requirements.txt, ignoring the lockfile. Dependencies diverge silently.
Free-threaded Python 3.13 has known correctness issues. Not production-ready in 2026.

antipattern.description: Applying Numba or Cython to I/O-bound loops.
antipattern.reason: JIT/AOT compilation speeds up CPU execution. A loop blocked on network/disk I/O is not CPU-bound — optimization yields no improvement.

rule.statement: Any change to lockfile, CI workflow, Dockerfile, or pyproject.toml [project] section requires human review.
rule.rationale: These files define the environment for all contributors and CI. Incorrect lockfile regeneration propagates across all machines on next sync.

template.purpose: Bootstrap a modern Python project: uv + ruff + mypy + pytest wired up
